narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 38—above the typical 36.7 average—your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 sits below the usual 3.21 mark, and your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, which the model considers alongside your plan to attend full-time like most students. The admissions model predicted a 75% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to gauge long-term success and avoid high-risk admits. This forecast stems from weighing various parts of your academic record against patterns from thousands of past students. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average 5.53, signaling weaker performance in later undergrad years compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the average 5.71, which reinforced concerns about early academic struggles that often carry over. While your stronger-than-average LSAT score helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing, it wasn't enough to offset those lower decile rankings in the model's eyes. Overall, your profile shows promise in test-taking ability but lags behind typical successful bar takers in consistent grade deciles, where stronger rankings are key predictors of first-try success. Many admitted students have higher deciles across the board, even with similar LSATs or backgrounds. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a modest family background—plenty overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes these grade trends for its bar pass forecast. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework, perhaps retaking undergrad classes or pursuing a post-bac program to demonstrate improvement. Consider reapplying after building a stronger transcript, and seek mentorship from bar-passed attorneys who share your background for tailored advice. With your LSAT edge, addressing those deciles could flip the prediction in your favor next time.
